blocks.content: Tuesday. English. Ms. Barrett asked us to write about a choice we'd made that week. Real or fictional, she said, but told us she could always tell which one it was.

blocks.speaker: Ms. Barrett
blocks.content: The best writing is when you're trying to understand something that happened to you, not just describe it. If you're not confused at all, it's probably not the real version.

I thought about Daniel. I thought about Mr. Cole's car. I thought about the corner at the end of Friday night. I had too many choices and I wasn't sure which one was the right one to write about because they all still felt unfinished.
I wrote about Thursday at the side exit. Not what I chose — just the moment before. The three seconds where I could see all the options and didn't know which one was the person I actually was.
I wrote: 'I don't think I know who I am when things get fast. I know who I want to be. But when it's actually happening the gap between those two things is very wide and very loud.'
Ms. Barrett collected them at the end of class. On the way out she said my name.
I didn't answer. But she was right.
